steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The question asks what Park et al.'s findings best support. Their key result: no significant variation in U-238 concentration or U-234/U-238 AR across multiple depths and locations within the same lake. The passage also establishes that non-uniform values can compromise sampling reliability.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — They illustrate why scientists investigating sediment transport and deposition and groundwater quality ought to evaluate how evenly U abundance and AR are distributed in the bodies of water they are examining.: This describes a general recommendation the passage background implies, not what Park et al.'s specific finding supports. Their result is about uniformity within their two lakes, not a prescription for all scientists. ✗
  • Choice B — They challenge the assumption that U abundance and AR sometimes vary from lake to lake, since the U-238 concentration and U-234/U-238 AR were consistent across the sampled lakes.: Park et al. studied variation within each lake, not between lakes. This misreads the scope of their finding. ✗
  • Choice C — They indicate that researchers performing isotopic analysis in the lakes Park et al. studied can likely rely on localized sampling within each lake, since values from any one location or depth are representative of the entire lake.: If values are consistent across all depths and locations within a lake, then any single sample is representative of the whole lake — localized sampling is reliable. This follows directly from their result. ✓
  • Choice D — They demonstrate that the number of samples Park et al. collected from the two lakes is sufficient to yield equally reliable information about sediment transport and deposition and groundwater quality in those two lakes.: The passage says nothing about sample count or whether information about sediment transport vs. groundwater quality is equally reliable. This goes beyond the data. ✗
That confirms C is correct.
